EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense lake effect snow band produced dangerous travel and near whiteout conditions. Snowfall totals exceeded a foot in portions of western Cass and Berrien counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single band of heavy lake effect developed behind an arctic front during the morning hours of January 14th, becoming nearly stationary and intensifying during the late afternoon through early morning hours of January 15th. Snowfall rates of more than 2 inches per hour were reported with near zero visibility. Snowfall totals were heaviest across far western portions of Cass County, with a report of 17.0 inches at Barron Lake. There were innumerous slide-offs and crashes across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event produced 3 to 6 inches of accumulation late January 24th into January 25th, heaviest during the morning hours of January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late evening hours of January 24th and continued through the early evening hours of January 25th. The snow accumulated to between 4 and 6 inches and was fluffy in nature as temperatures were in the single digits and teens. Roads were snow covered and slick with reports of accidents and slide-offs across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event produced 3 to 6 inches of accumulation late January 24th into January 25th, heaviest during the morning hours of January 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late evening hours of January 24th and continued through the early evening hours of January 25th. The snow accumulated to between 3 and 5 inches and was fluffy in nature as temperatures were in the single digits and teens. Roads were snow covered and slick with reports of accidents and slide-offs across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: System snow overspread southern Lower Michigan during the evening hours of January 18th which was followed by periods of lake effect snow on January 19th behind an arctic front. Storm total snowfall accumulations ranged from 2 to 6 inches across southwest Lower Michigan with the greatest amounts reported closer to Lake Michigan.  Strong winds coupled with the snow and blowing snow produced significant visibility reductions at times and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light system snow during the late afternoon and evening of January 18th was followed by bands of lake effect snow. The combination of west wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and the snowfall resulted in significant visibility reductions of less than one quarter of a mile at times due to snow and blowing snow. Storm total snow accumulations were in the 2 to 5 inch range. A report of 3.4 inches of snow was received 2.8 miles north northwest of Edwardsburg, MI. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region. In addition, wind chills dropped to as cold as 15 below during the early morning hours of January 19th extending into early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: System snow overspread southern Lower Michigan during the evening hours of January 18th which was followed by periods of lake effect snow on January 19th behind an arctic front. Storm total snowfall accumulations ranged from 2 to 6 inches across southwest Lower Michigan with the greatest amounts reported closer to Lake Michigan.  Strong winds coupled with the snow and blowing snow produced significant visibility reductions at times and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light system snow during the late afternoon and evening of January 18th was followed by bands of lake effect snow. The combination of west wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and the snowfall resulted in significant visibility reductions at times due to snow and blowing snow. While storm total snow accumulations were only reported in the 1 to 2 inch range, the combination of snow and blowing snow produced hazardous travel conditions. The heaviest snowfall rates of the event occurred during the early morning of January 19th as a dominant band of snow tracked across the county. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region. In addition, wind chills dropped to as cold as 15 below during the early morning hours of January 19th extending into early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain passed over the central UP prior to the morning commute of the 13th, causing delays and closures of schools as well as minor travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple CoCoRaHS sites reported between 0.01 and 0.05 inches of freezing rain accumulation. Additionally, schools in the southern portion of the county were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain passed over the central UP prior to the morning commute of the 13th, causing delays and closures of schools as well as minor travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS station KIMT at Ford Airport reported 0.09 inches of ice accumulation. Key impacts: widespread school delays of 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain passed over the central UP prior to the morning commute of the 13th, causing delays and closures of schools as well as minor travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported that freezing rain accumulation caused a 2 hour delay for schools in the west part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain passed over the central UP prior to the morning commute of the 13th, causing delays and closures of schools as well as minor travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported that freezing rain forced a two hour delay of some schools in central portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure tracking through Lower Michigan caused heavy snowfall to affect much of central Upper Michigan beginning on the 10th and lasting through the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of heavy snowfall in the county include a CoCoRaHS report of 8.7 inches of snow in Spalding, a public report in Cedar River of 9 inches, and a trained spotter report 2 miles east of Daggett of 7.5 inches. Key impacts: Difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure tracking through Lower Michigan caused heavy snowfall to affect much of central Upper Michigan beginning on the 10th and lasting through the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of heavy snowfall in the county include the CO-OP observer HERM4 2 SE Herman reporting 10 inches of snow, a public report 5 miles southeast of Mount Arvon reporting 8 inches of snow, and a trained spotter report of 6.9 inches 2 miles south of Baraga, with that report noting most fell from 11 AM on the 10th to midnight the 10th into the 11th. Key impacts: difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass entered the UP late on the 22nd, with very cold wind chills of -25F or lower persisting at times through the morning of the 26th. The coldest wind chills occurred during the mornings of the 23rd and 24th, when minimum values ranged from -30 to -55F in the central and western UP, and -25 to -25F in the eastern UP. For some areas in the central and western UP, this was the coldest 3 and 4 day stretch since January 2019. Numerous schools, businesses, and services were closed on Friday the 23rd and Saturday the 24th.||In addition to the cold temperatures, blizzard conditions accompanied the initial push of cold air during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd in the Keweenaw, with whiteout conditions leading to several traffic accidents and road closures. Another round of heavy accumulating lake effect snow occurred in the northeastern UP on the 23rd, with very low visibilities resulting in at least one road closure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds, accumulating snow, and cold temperatures resulted in blizzard conditions during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd. Key impacts: traffic accidents, transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wing of warm advection snow overspread the area during the day of the 10th, with a mesolow crossing the Leelanau Peninsula and tracking near the M-72 corridor during the evening, leading to localized snowfall totals as high as 10-12 inches where N to NNW flow lake enhancement came into play across parts of Leelanau, Benzie, and Grand Traverse Counties, as well as into the Kalkaska county area. Meanwhile, a convergence band developed ahead of this mesolow, resulting in 7-8 inches of snowfall across parts of northeast Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimate of 7 inches of snowfall in the city of Cheboygan between 4 and 9pm with some blowing and drifting and continued snowfall through the night. Additional reports of around 7 inches of snow in the Black Lake area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer measured 7.0 of snow for the storm total by the morning of the 11th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -40F were observed on the 23rd and 24th, and as low as -25F on the 25th and 26th. Key impacts: school and business closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wing of warm advection snow overspread the area during the day of the 10th, with a mesolow crossing the Leelanau Peninsula and tracking near the M-72 corridor during the evening, leading to localized snowfall totals as high as 10-12 inches where N to NNW flow lake enhancement came into play across parts of Leelanau, Benzie, and Grand Traverse Counties, as well as into the Kalkaska county area. Meanwhile, a convergence band developed ahead of this mesolow, resulting in 7-8 inches of snowfall across parts of northeast Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall observer near Honor reported 8 inches of snow by 11pm on the 10th; snowfall totals in the Honor area may have been as high as 10-12inches. Additional reports of up to 9 inches in the Lake Ann area in the eastern half of the county.

For some areas in the central and western UP, this was the coldest 3 and 4 day stretch since January 2019. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado low moved through the Upper Great Lakes on January 9th bringing wet snow to parts of the western and north central U.P. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: System snow overspread southern Lower Michigan during the evening hours of January 18th which was followed by periods of lake effect snow on January 19th behind an arctic front. Storm total snowfall accumulations ranged from 2 to 6 inches across southwest Lower Michigan with the greatest amounts reported closer to Lake Michigan.  Strong winds coupled with the snow and blowing snow produced significant visibility reductions at times and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light system snow during the late afternoon and evening of January 18th was followed by bands of lake effect snow. The combination of west wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph and the snowfall resulted in significant visibility reductions of less than one quarter of a mile at times due to snow and blowing snow. Storm total snow accumulations were in the 2 to 6 inch range. A report of 5.2 inches of snow was received 2.8 miles south southeast of St. Joseph, MI. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region. In addition, wind chills dropped to as cold as 15 below during the early morning hours of January 19th extending into early afternoon.

The heaviest snowfall rates of the event occurred during the early morning of January 19th as a dominant band of snow tracked across the county. Numerous slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region. In addition, wind chills dropped to as cold as 15 below during the early morning hours of January 19th extending into early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense band of lake effect snow produced near whiteout conditions and 2 to 4 inch per hour snowfall rates during the late evening hours of January 25th into the morning of January 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving, lake effect snow band produced treacherous, nearly impossible travel due to intense snowfall rates and near zero visibility late January 25th into the morning hours of January 26th. Snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour were reported, with snowfall totals generally ranging between 8 and 14 inches across the county. There was a report of 12.7 inches 1 mile south of Buchanan. Numerous schools and businesses were closed on January 26th with reports of slide-offs and accidents on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper low moved through the region bringing widespread light snow to the U.P. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow developed over the northern and western U.P. as an arctic air mass moved into the Great Lakes, disrupting travel and resulting in school closures. In addition to snowfall, winds and arctic air brought very cold windchills in the -20s across the U.P.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow and blowing snow brought periods of visibility down to 1/4 mile at the Houghton County Aiport ASOS (KCMX). 6 to 12 of snowfall was reported with the Kearsarge CO-OP reporting 8 of snow in 24 hours at 0800 EST on 1/19.  Twin Lakes CoCoRaHS reported 12.3 of snowfall in 24 hours at 0600 ES 1/19. Key impacts: Tranportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures bottoming out around zero during Friday morning, coupled with wind speeds in excess of 15 mph, produced wind chills of 15 to 25 below zero across most of southeast Michigan. Even slightly colder readings were noted across Oakland, Genesee, Saginaw, and Tuscola counties. The arctic airmass persisted into Saturday morning, with renewed wind chill drops to around 30 degrees below zero across Midland, Saginaw, Tuscola, Genesee, Livingston, and Washtenaw counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intoxicated 19 year male student left a frat party in Ann Arbor around 1 AM Friday Jan 23rd and was later found dead outside. The autopsy report stated the cause of his death as hypothermia, and described the manner of death as accidental.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper low moved through the region bringing widespread light snow to the U.P. Moderate to heavy lake effect snow developed over the northern and western U.P. as an arctic air mass moved into the Great Lakes, disrupting travel and resulting in school closures. In addition to snowfall, winds and arctic air brought very cold windchills in the -20s across the U.P.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow brought periods of visibility as low as 1/4 mile to the Ironwood Airport (IWD, nearest ASOS). Reported snow accumulations were sparse and below traditional criteria but heavy snow and blowing snow were impactful. 12.3 of snowfall in 24 hours was reported 3.5 miles east of Ontonagon County in Houghton County (3-14-2026 0600 EST). Key Impacts: Transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure tracking through Lower Michigan caused heavy snowfall to affect much of central Upper Michigan beginning on the 10th and lasting through the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall was recorded in many locations in Marquette County, including the official NWS Marquette observation in Negaunee Township reaching 8 inches by midnight on the 10th into the 11th and a storm total of 10.3 inches. The highest reports were a public report 2 miles west of Ishpeming of 12 inches and the 1 NNE Arnold CO-OP Observer also reporting 12 inches of snow. Key impacts: difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the U.P. during the morning hours on Jan 14th, associated with a clipper low that passed north of Lake Superior.  Strong winds and heavy lake effect snow behind the front brought blizzard conditions to Marquette and Alger Counties.  Periods of near blizzard conditions were observed in the Keweenaw Peninsula.  Law enforcement reported near zero visibility and the runway at Sawyer Regional Airport was closed.  Snowfall amounts of 5 to 10 inches were reported across the northern half of Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility down to 1/4 mile at Sawyer Regional Airport (KSAW).  Munising ASOS KP53 reported frequent gusts over 40 mph and as high as 50 mph.  Winds were highest near the lakeshore.  Munising delayed the start of school by 3 hours.  Key Impacts: Transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the U.P. during the morning hours on Jan 14th, associated with a clipper low that passed north of Lake Superior.  Strong winds and heavy lake effect snow behind the front brought blizzard conditions to Marquette and Alger Counties.  Periods of near blizzard conditions were observed in the Keweenaw Peninsula.  Law enforcement reported near zero visibility and the runway at Sawyer Regional Airport was closed.  Snowfall amounts of 5 to 10 inches were reported across the northern half of Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility down to 1/4 mile at Sawyer Regional Airport ASOS (KSAW) but wind gusts were not reporting. Munising ASOS KP53 reported frequent gusts over 40 mph and as high as 50 mph. Winds were highest over the northern half of Marquette County with the Harvey MDOT reporting frequent gusts over 50 mph and as high as 61 mph. There were many closures including the larger school districts and Northern Michigan University. Forsyth Police Dept reported near zero visibility at M553 and M94 and the township issued a statement to not travel. Key Impacts: Transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the U.P. during the morning hours on Jan 14th, associated with a clipper low that passed north of Lake Superior.  Strong winds and heavy lake effect snow behind the front brought blizzard conditions to Marquette and Alger Counties.  Periods of near blizzard conditions were observed in the Keweenaw Peninsula.  Law enforcement reported near zero visibility and the runway at Sawyer Regional Airport was closed.  Snowfall amounts of 5 to 10 inches were reported across the northern half of Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Houghton County Airport (KCMX) reported visibility of 1/4 mile for over 3 hours as heavy lake effect snow moved through the Keweenaw resulting in near-blizzard conditions. Winds gusted as high as 49 mph. Key Impacts: Transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the U.P. during the morning hours on Jan 14th, associated with a clipper low that passed north of Lake Superior.  Strong winds and heavy lake effect snow behind the front brought blizzard conditions to Marquette and Alger Counties.  Periods of near blizzard conditions were observed in the Keweenaw Peninsula.  Law enforcement reported near zero visibility and the runway at Sawyer Regional Airport was closed.  Snowfall amounts of 5 to 10 inches were reported across the northern half of Marquette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Houghton County Airport (KCMX) reported visibility of 1/4 mile for over 3 hours as heavy lake effect snow moved through the Keweenaw resulting in near-blizzard conditions. Winds gusted as high as 49 mph. Key Impacts: Transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low over Ontario allowed record warmth to spread into southeast Michigan, as Detroit Metro Airport reached 60 degrees. This warmth and a dry slot moving through allowed for good mixing, leading to wind gusts of 45-55 mph across most of southeast Michigan. Localized peak winds around 65 mph were noted across parts of the Detroit Metro area, leading to some reports of tree and power line damage. The combination of these high winds and saturated ground from the morning rainfall led to reports of uprooted trees as well. Over 25,000 residents lost power, with the bulk residing in Oakland, Wayne, and Macomb counties.

A very dry and frigid air mass preceded a long-duration snowstorm that impacted Southeast Michigan on Sunday, January 25. Because much of the region already had 2 to 6 inches of snow on the ground, obtaining accurate new snowfall reports from the public proved challenging.||Southeast Michigan sat on the northern fringe of a major winter system that impacted most of the eastern half of the United States. Due to the lingering cold air, snowflake size remained small as temperatures only rose into the mid-teens by Sunday evening. Consequently, the majority of Southeast Michigan saw 2 to 5 inches of dry, powdery snow by the time the system exited on Monday morning, January 26.||Higher totals occurred in two specific areas:|The Southern Border: Better moisture near the Michigan-Ohio border allowed parts of Monroe County to see 7+ inches.|The Eastern Thumb: Northeast winds off Lake Huron added localized moisture, resulting in isolated totals of 7 to 8.5 inches near the lakeshore of St. Clair County.|Conversely, the dry air held strongest across Midland, Bay, and Saginaw counties, where totals remained largely under 2 inches.

Temperatures bottoming out around zero during Friday morning, coupled with wind speeds in excess of 15 mph, produced wind chills of 15 to 25 below zero across most of southeast Michigan. Even slightly colder readings were noted across Oakland, Genesee, Saginaw, and Tuscola counties. The arctic airmass persisted into Saturday morning, with renewed wind chill drops to around 30 degrees below zero across Midland, Saginaw, Tuscola, Genesee, Livingston, and Washtenaw counties.

Strong lake effect snow showers occurred January 28 through January 29th, impacting Luce, Schoolcraft, and Alger counties in Upper Michigan. As the event progressed, a dominant lake effect band slowly progressed westward, which eventually focused impacts into Alger County. Heavy snowfall was observed, with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour or greater and gusty northwest winds contributing to blowing snow and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby observation from Chatham Cooperative Observer (CEFM4) observed 8 inches of snow between 0800 1/28 and 1/29 and the NOHRSC snowfall analysis highlights similar snowfall amounts in northern Schoolcraft County. Key impacts: Difficult travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure moved across the Upper Great Lakes on Jan 16-17 bringing moderate to heavy lake effect snow to the western U.P.  The heaviest snow fell across Gogebic County with 6-10 of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy lake effect snow fell over much of Ontonagon County on Jan 16-17 with snowfall up to 10.5 at 5 NNW Bessemer, MI-GG-11, and the NOHRSC analysis showing 8-12 extending into much of the western half of Ontonagon County:  Key impacts: transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass entered the UP late on the 22nd, with very cold wind chills of -25F or lower persisting at times through the morning of the 26th. The coldest wind chills occurred during the mornings of the 23rd and 24th, when minimum values ranged from -30 to -55F in the central and western UP, and -25 to -25F in the eastern UP. For some areas in the central and western UP, this was the coldest 3 and 4 day stretch since January 2019. Numerous schools, businesses, and services were closed on Friday the 23rd and Saturday the 24th.||In addition to the cold temperatures, blizzard conditions accompanied the initial push of cold air during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd in the Keweenaw, with whiteout conditions leading to several traffic accidents and road closures. Another round of heavy accumulating lake effect snow occurred in the northeastern UP on the 23rd, with very low visibilities resulting in at least one road closure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds, accumulating snow, and cold temperatures resulted in blizzard conditions during the afternoon and evening of the 22nd. US-41 was closed in at least two spots north of Houghton due to traffic accidents and whiteout conditions. There was an 8-car pileup near the Houghton County Airport, and multiple smaller accidents. Key impacts: traffic accidents, transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SW flow lake effect snow developed late in the day on the 18th, and a mesolow dropped SSE across the area on the morning of the 19th, crossing from Leelanau County to near Grayling through the morning hours. A strong NW flow lake effect band developed in its wake with flow ultimately shifting to the WNW in the late afternoon/evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 9 inches of snow fell between midnight and 9am on the 19th, with a 48-hr total of 16.7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalous cold air settled into the eastern US for late January, with the coldest period for the Upper Great Lakes from the night of Jan 22nd through the morning of the 24th. Actual air temperatures struggling to break 0 degrees Fahrenheit, combined with winds gusting as high as 15-25mph at times, resulted in extreme cold apparent temperatures of -15 to -25F, and -25 to -35F during the overnight hours on the 22nd into the 23rd, and the 23rd into the 24th. The 23rd into the 24th was the coldest of the two nights. Strong gusty winds and some snowfall resulted in significant blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities to whiteout conditions at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apparent temperatures at KMCD, Mackinac Island AWOS, as low as -34F on the night of the 22nd-23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wing of warm advection snow overspread the area during the day of the 10th, with a mesolow crossing the Leelanau Peninsula and tracking near the M-72 corridor during the evening, leading to localized snowfall totals as high as 10-12 inches where N to NNW flow lake enhancement came into play across parts of Leelanau, Benzie, and Grand Traverse Counties, as well as into the Kalkaska county area. Meanwhile, a convergence band developed ahead of this mesolow, resulting in 7-8 inches of snowfall across parts of northeast Lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer near Grawn reported 9 inches of snow by the morning of the 11th, with additional reports of up to 9 inches near Interlochen.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimate of up to 10 inches on the south side of Big Glen Lake by 11am, combined with wind gusts as high as 58 mph measured at Leland Harbor during the morning hours of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalous cold air settled into the eastern US for late January, with the coldest period for the Upper Great Lakes from the night of Jan 22nd through the morning of the 24th. Actual air temperatures struggling to break 0 degrees Fahrenheit, combined with winds gusting as high as 15-25mph at times, resulted in extreme cold apparent temperatures of -15 to -25F, and -25 to -35F during the overnight hours on the 22nd into the 23rd, and the 23rd into the 24th. The 23rd into the 24th was the coldest of the two nights. Strong gusty winds and some snowfall resulted in significant blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities to whiteout conditions at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS-maintained equipment at Point Iroquois light reported a wind chill as low as -26F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shot of arctic air punched into the region on the evening of the 28th into the morning of the 29th, with cold temperatures depending mainly on radiational cooling combined with light winds to drop wind chills to around -15 to -20F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS site near Alger in western Arenac county reported a wind chill of -18F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SW flow lake effect snow developed late in the day on the 18th, and a mesolow dropped SSE across the area on the morning of the 19th, crossing from Leelanau County to near Grayling through the morning hours. A strong NW flow lake effect band developed in its wake with flow ultimately shifting to the WNW in the late afternoon/evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall observer near Brethren in southern Manistee county reported 8 inches by the evening of the 19th, with a 21 to 1 snow-to-liquid ratio; 10.2 inches of snow fell over 30 to 36 hrs near Wellston. Winds gusted as high as 45mph at Manistee-Blacker Airport in Manistee during the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong lake effect snow showers occurred January 28 through January 29th, impacting Luce, Schoolcraft, and Alger counties in Upper Michigan. As the event progressed, a dominant lake effect band slowly progressed westward, which eventually focused impacts into Alger County. Heavy snowfall was observed, with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour or greater and gusty northwest winds contributing to blowing snow and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chatham Cooperative Observer (CEFM4) observed 8 inches of snow between 0800 1/28 and 1/29. Impacts for this event began on 1/27, when it was also reported that a hill along M-28 in Wetmore was closed because a traffic incident involving a semi. Frequent whiteouts were also reported on M-28 between Harvey and Munising and Alger County Sheriff advised that people should stay home and not travel unless absolutely necessary. It was reported that Munising schools had closed early. Key impacts: Difficult to dangerous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of light freezing rain moved west to east across the UP during the afternoon and evening of the 6th into the early morning hours of the 7th. Although amounts were light, a glaze of ice resulted in treacherous travel conditions on untreated roadways. A few traffic accidents were reported, including a school bus carrying a high school girls' basketball team that flipped on its side between Marquette and K.I. Sawyer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain resulted in a glaze of ice on untreated surfaces. Key impacts: transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado low moved through the Upper Great Lakes on January 9th bringing wet snow to parts of the western and north central U.P.  Over the eastern U.P. precipitation fell as rain with widespread amounts over 1.25 inches.  This resulted in ponding of water on the roads in the Escanaba area.  Snow amounts were generally in the 3 to 5 in range but as much as 9 inches was reported in Baraga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet heavy snow fell over the northern half of Marquette County resulting in travel difficulties and school closures, including the larger school districts.  Snow amounts were generally in the 3 to 4 inch range, though near the lakeshore there was considerably less snow.  The NWS Negaunee office reported 4.1 of snowfall with this event on 1/9, 0658 EST. Key impacts: Transportation delays, school closures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch or two of wet snow along with rainfall of over an inch resulted in slushy roads around Alger County.  The Munising School District closed due to the slushy conditions. Key impacts: Transportation delays, school closures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of heavy snow resulted in 3 to 6 inches of accumulation and snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour. Key impacts: transportation delays, school closures.
